topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my phone, a jarring intrusion into an otherwise quiet Tuesday afternoon. My stomach clenched, a familiar tightening that always accompanied unwelcome surprises. It was a request from Mark. Mark, the architect of my awkward middle school years, the one whose shadow I’d tried, and failed, to outrun. I stared at the screen, my thumb hovering over the ‘decline’ button. A wave of coldness washed over me, making the air in the room feel suddenly thin. Why now? After all this time, after the silence? I reread the username, a slow, deliberate movement of my eyes tracing the letters. A muscle twitched in my jaw. I didn't open the chat. I just locked the screen and walked to the window, watching the rain streak down the glass, trying to convince myself it was a glitch, a prank, anything but the ghost of my past leaning in.

[story 2]

I picked up my phone, the glass cool against my palm. The profile picture was grainy, taken from some distant, questionable vacation spot. Mark. The name hit me like a dull thud. My breath hitched, a shallow intake of air. I scrolled through his past interactions—mostly empty, aggressive exchanges from years ago. My fingers tapped a rapid, uneven rhythm on the screen. A low hum of unease vibrated through my chest. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, trying to build a wall of indifference. This felt manufactured. Too neat. I considered deleting the app entirely, but a strange compulsion kept my finger hovering near the ‘accept’ button. What was he actually trying to achieve? I took a deep, steadying breath, forcing my expression into something neutral, even as my heart hammered against my ribs like a trapped bird.

[story 3]

The sound of the notification was sharp, cutting through the low murmur of the coffee shop. I didn't look up immediately; I was focused on stirring my lukewarm latte, the ceramic mug warm in my hands. When I finally glanced at the screen, a distinct feeling of internal resistance flared up. It was him. The boy who used to relish making me feel small. A faint tremor ran through my hand as I brought the phone closer. I studied the request—a simple ‘friend request.’ My lips pressed into a thin, unreadable line. I took a sip of coffee, letting the bitterness settle, trying to anchor myself in the present moment. Every instinct screamed at me to ignore it, to let the digital invitation dissolve into nothingness. But the inertia was powerful; I felt compelled to see what this sudden reappearance would bring.
